overview: Private equity is an asset class in which capital is invested directly into private companies or in buyouts of public companies, with the goal of improving them operationally and financially before selling at a profit. PE firms raise money from institutional investors such as pension funds, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds, then deploy that capital through structured fund vehicles over a defined lifecycle. Understanding the mechanics of fund structure, deal execution, value creation, and exit strategy is essential for anyone pursuing a career in PE or advising PE clients in investment banking. This module covers the full arc of the private equity industry from fundraising through exit.

lessons.title: Private Equity Industry Overview

blocks.text: What Is Private Equity?

Private equity (PE) refers to investment funds that buy ownership stakes in companies that are not publicly traded on a stock exchange. The core premise is straightforward: acquire a company (or a controlling interest), improve its operations and financial profile over a multi-year hold period, and sell it at a higher price than you paid. The difference between the purchase price and the sale price — after accounting for debt repayment, fees, and the cost of capital — is the return that flows back to investors.

PE firms target companies across a wide range of sectors and sizes, from mid-market businesses generating $10-50M in EBITDA to mega-cap buyouts exceeding $10 billion in enterprise value. Blackstone's acquisition of Hilton Hotels in 2007 for $26 billion remains one of the most celebrated PE deals ever, ultimately generating over $14 billion in profit when Hilton went public in 2013. This single deal illustrates the potential scale of PE returns when operational improvement, financial engineering, and favorable market conditions align.
PE Strategies: Not All PE Is the Same

The term 'private equity' is used loosely to describe several distinct investment strategies. Understanding these differences is essential for interviews at PE firms, which often specialize in one strategy.

blocks.caption: PE Strategy Comparison
rows: General Atlantic, Summit Partners, TA Associates
Early-stage startups; pre-revenue or early revenue
Companies in or near financial distress
Profitable but growing; hybrid of LBO and growth equity
Vista Equity, Francisco Partners, Thoma Bravo

blocks.text: PE vs. Other Asset Classes

The critical distinction between PE and venture capital is profitability. PE firms buy companies that already generate positive EBITDA and use debt to amplify equity returns. VC firms bet on pre-revenue or early-stage startups where the path to profitability is uncertain. Growth equity sits in the middle: investing in companies that are growing fast but not yet using leverage, with a minority stake and no operational overhaul required.
Fund Structure: LP and GP

Private equity funds are structured as limited partnerships (LPs). This legal structure divides responsibilities and economics between two parties: the General Partner (GP) and the Limited Partners (LPs). Understanding this structure is foundational to every other concept in PE.

items: General Partner (GP): The PE firm itself — e.g., KKR, Carlyle, Bain Capital. The GP sources deals, manages the portfolio, makes investment decisions, and is responsible for the fund's performance. GPs typically commit 1-5% of the fund's total capital from their own balance sheet (called the GP commitment or 'GP-co investment'), aligning their interests with LPs.

Limited Partners (LPs): The institutional and high-net-worth investors who provide the majority of capital. LPs have limited liability — they cannot lose more than their committed capital — and take a passive role in investment decisions. Common LPs include public pension funds (CalPERS, Ontario Teachers'), university endowments (Harvard, Yale), sovereign wealth funds (GIC, Abu Dhabi Investment Authority), insurance companies, and family offices.

Limited Partnership Agreement (LPA): The governing legal document that defines all economic terms, governance rights, fee structures, and distribution rules. Every provision of the GP-LP relationship flows from the LPA.

blocks.text: Fund Economics: '2 and 20'

The standard fee structure in private equity — '2 and 20' — refers to a 2% annual management fee and 20% carried interest (carry). These two components represent the two ways GPs generate revenue: a steady base fee for running the fund, and a performance fee for generating returns above a threshold.

blocks.caption: PE Fund Fee Economics: $1 Billion Fund Example
rows: Invested capital or NAV post-investment period
GP invests own money alongside LPs

blocks.title: GP Carry Earned at Different Fund Returns ($1B Fund)
blocks.subtitle: Gross return scenarios with 8% hurdle, 20% carry above hurdle

blocks.question: A PE fund has $1B AUM and earns a 2% management fee annually. Over 5 years it generates a 3x gross MoM. How much carry does the GP earn, assuming 20% carry above an 8% hurdle rate? Show the full waterfall calculation.
blocks.answer: Step 1 — Management fees: 2% x $1B x 5 years = $100M in management fees (paid out to GP regardless). Step 2 — Total gross proceeds: 3x MoM on $1B = $3B total proceeds from exits. Step 3 — Distribution waterfall. Return of capital: LPs receive $1B back first. Remaining proceeds: $3B - $1B = $2B. Preferred return (8% compounded over 5 years): $1B x (1.08^5 - 1) = $1B x 0.469 = $469M owed to LPs as preferred return. Since $2B remains, LPs receive their $469M pref. Remaining after pref: $2B - $469M = $1.531B. GP catch-up: GP must 'catch up' to receive 20% of total profits ($2B total profit x 20% = $400M). GP has earned $0 so far, so it receives catch-up distributions until GP has received $400M total carry (or until catch-up pool exhausted). Catch-up pool: $1.531B. GP catch-up = min($400M, $1.531B x [catch-up rate]). At a 100% catch-up rate, GP receives up to $400M before the 80/20 split kicks in. GP takes $400M in catch-up (leaving $1.131B). Remaining split (80/20): LPs receive 80% x $1.131B = $905M. GP receives 20% x $1.131B = $226M additional carry. Total GP carry: $400M (catch-up) - but wait, we need to net back to the true 20% split. Total profits = $2B. GP carry = 20% x $2B = $400M gross carry. LP receives: $1B (capital) + $469M (pref) + 80% x ($2B - $469M) = $1B + $469M + $1.225B = $2.694B. GP receives: $400M carry + $100M management fees = $500M total gross compensation.

blocks.text: The Distribution Waterfall

Before GPs can earn carried interest, distributions follow a strict waterfall — a contractual pecking order written into the LPA. The European (whole-fund) waterfall is now standard for large buyout funds and is the model tested most frequently in interviews.

items: Return of Capital: LPs get 100% of distributions until all invested capital is fully returned. On a $1B fund, LPs must receive back their $1B before any carry is calculated.

Preferred Return (Hurdle Rate): LPs receive a preferred return — typically 8% per annum compounded — on their invested capital before the GP earns any carry. This is the LPs' 'minimum acceptable return' for taking the risk.

GP Catch-Up: Once LPs receive their 8% preferred return, the GP receives a disproportionately large share of the next distributions (often 80-100%) until it has caught up to its 20% share of total profits. This 'catches up' the GP to its carried interest entitlement.
Carried Interest Split: After the catch-up, remaining profits are split 80% to LPs / 20% to GP (or sometimes 85/15 or 70/30 for newer or smaller funds).

blocks.title: Waterfall Worked Example
blocks.text: Fund size: $1B. Fund exits all investments, total proceeds: $1.8B. Step 1 — Return of capital: LPs receive $1B back. Remaining: $800M. Step 2 — Preferred return: 8% on $1B for 5 years = ~$469M owed to LPs. But only $800M remains, so LPs receive $469M. Remaining: $331M. Step 3 — GP catch-up: GP receives ~$117M (to reach 20% of $586M total profit). Remaining: ~$214M. Step 4 — Carry split: LPs get 80% ($171M), GP gets 20% ($43M). Total GP carry: ~$160M. Total LP proceeds: ~$1.64B.
Fund Lifecycle and Performance Metrics

A typical PE fund has a 10-year term with possible 1-2 year extensions. The lifecycle breaks into distinct phases: fundraising (12-24 months), the investment period during which new deals are made (3-5 years from fund close), the hold period during which portfolio companies are managed and improved, and the harvest period during which exits occur and capital is returned to LPs.

blocks.caption: PE Fund Performance Metrics
rows: Cash returned to LPs / LP capital contributed
Realized returns — 'cash on cash'. DPI > 1x means LPs have gotten their money back.
NAV of remaining portfolio / LP capital contributed
Unrealized paper value of remaining investments
Total estimated return, both realized and unrealized. Also called MOIC (Multiple of Invested Capital).
Annualized time-weighted return on invested capital
Time-adjusted return; favored by LPs who can compare across asset classes
Year the fund made its first investment
Used to benchmark funds against peers raised in similar market conditions

blocks.text: Major PE Firms

items: Blackstone ($1T+ AUM): World's largest alternative asset manager. Known for real estate, credit, and buyouts. IPO'd in 2007.
KKR ($600B+ AUM): Pioneered the LBO with the 1989 RJR Nabisco deal ($25B — the largest buyout of its era). Strong in infrastructure, credit, and growth equity.
Apollo Global Management ($650B+ AUM): Known for distressed and credit investing as well as traditional PE. Merged with Athene in 2022.
Carlyle Group ($425B+ AUM): Historically strong in defense/government-related businesses. Global footprint with offices in 30+ countries.
Bain Capital ($185B+ AUM): Co-founded by Mitt Romney. Strong consumer, healthcare, and technology focus. Known for operational rigor.
TPG ($224B+ AUM): Healthcare and technology specialist. IPO'd in 2022. Known for creative deal structures.

blocks.title: Key Insight: Why LP-GP Alignment Matters
blocks.text: The '2 and 20' model creates a potential misalignment: GPs earn management fees regardless of performance, which can incentivize them to raise larger and larger funds (more fees) even if deployment becomes harder. The preferred return / hurdle rate is the primary mechanism to realign incentives — GPs earn carry only after LPs have received an 8% return. In down markets or for underperforming funds, GPs may operate for years earning only management fees with no carry in sight, which is why top firms focus obsessively on building track records of consistent top-quartile returns.

The GP earns meaningful wealth not through management fees — which merely pay operating costs — but through carried interest, which can be tens or hundreds of millions of dollars on a successful fund. This is why alignment with LPs through the hurdle rate and GP commitment is the cornerstone of every LP-GP relationship.

keyTakeaways: PE funds are structured as limited partnerships with GPs managing investments and LPs providing capital; the fund lifecycle spans ~10 years from fundraising to final exit.

The '2 and 20' fee model (2% management fee, 20% carried interest) is standard, with the distribution waterfall ensuring LPs receive capital back plus an 8% preferred return before GPs earn any carry.
PE differs from VC and growth equity primarily through the use of leverage, majority control, and focus on profitable, mature businesses.
Key performance metrics are DPI, RVPI, TVPI/MOIC, and IRR — understanding these is essential for PE interviews.
Top firms like Blackstone, KKR, Apollo, and Carlyle manage hundreds of billions in AUM and operate across multiple asset classes beyond traditional buyouts.
lessons.interviewTip: When asked 'walk me through how PE fund economics work,' structure your answer as: (1) LP/GP structure and who provides capital, (2) the '2 and 20' fee model, (3) the distribution waterfall in four steps — return of capital, preferred return, catch-up, carry split. Interviewers love when candidates can walk through a numerical waterfall example on the fly — practice with a $1B fund, $1.8B exit, 5-year hold.

lessons.title: The PE Investment Process

blocks.text: Deal Sourcing: Finding the Opportunity

For a PE firm, the investment process begins long before any financial model is built — it starts with finding the deal. Deal sourcing is the identification and initial evaluation of potential acquisition targets, and it is one of the most important differentiators between top-performing and average PE firms. The average PE firm reviews approximately 80 opportunities for every single investment it actually completes.

items: Proprietary Sourcing: The PE firm proactively contacts company management or owners before a formal sale process begins. Proprietary deals allow PE firms to avoid competitive auctions, negotiate directly, and often acquire at lower valuations. Requires a large network of relationships — management teams, advisors, industry executives, and intermediaries.

Banker-Run Processes: Investment banks run formal sale processes (auctions) on behalf of sellers. These include broad auctions (20-50+ bidders), targeted processes (5-15 bidders), and bilateral negotiations. PE firms receive a Confidential Information Memorandum (CIM) or 'book' from the bank describing the business and financial information.
Secondary Buyouts: Acquiring companies from other PE sponsors who are ready to exit. Common in today's market as PE inventory continues to grow.

Carve-Outs: Buying a division or subsidiary from a larger corporation. Carve-outs often trade at a discount because they lack standalone infrastructure and require more operational work post-acquisition.

blocks.question: What is the difference between a proprietary deal and a banker-run process, and why do PE firms strongly prefer proprietary sourcing? What are the realistic limits of proprietary sourcing?
blocks.answer: In a proprietary deal, the PE firm approaches a company directly — often through a relationship built over months or years with the founder, CEO, or board — before any banker has been engaged. The firm negotiates bilaterally, often at a price that reflects the absence of competitive tension. In a banker-run process, the investment bank solicits bids from 15-50 potential buyers simultaneously, creating an auction dynamic that drives prices to the highest competitive level. PE firms prefer proprietary deals for three reasons: (1) Price: without auction dynamics, prices tend to be 1-3x lower on EBITDA multiple — meaning a firm might pay 8x vs. 11x for the same business. This dramatically improves return prospects. (2) Information advantage: in a bilateral process, the PE firm can spend more time building rapport with management and gaining comfort on nuanced issues that are hard to analyze in a 6-week banker auction. (3) Relationship capital: the company's management team tends to prefer a buyer who approached them thoughtfully over one that won a commoditized auction. The limits of proprietary sourcing: most sizeable ($500M+ enterprise value) deals are run by bankers because sellers understand their market power and hire advisors to maximize proceeds. True proprietary deals are increasingly concentrated in the lower middle market ($25-150M EBITDA) where bankers are less involved and founder-owned businesses still dominate.

blocks.title: PE Deal Process: Sourcing to Close
steps: Deal Sourcing: Proprietary outreach or banker CIM received; initial review against fund mandate criteria
Initial Screen (1-2 weeks): Deal team builds high-level financial model; investment thesis drafted; go/no-go decision
Management Presentation: NDA signed; company management presents business; preliminary questions answered
LOI / Indication of Interest: PE firm submits non-binding letter of intent with indicative price range and deal structure
Due Diligence (6-12 weeks): Parallel commercial, financial, legal, management, IT, and ESG workstreams; QofE report from Big 4
Investment Committee Approval: Final IC presentation with full financial model, risk analysis, and return scenarios; approval to sign
Financing Commitment: Leveraged finance banks commit to debt package; commitment letters signed
SPA Negotiation and Signing: Definitive purchase agreement signed; reps and warranties negotiated; R&W insurance obtained
Regulatory Approvals: HSR antitrust clearance; other regulatory filings as needed (CFIUS, industry-specific)
Closing: Funds transferred; shares acquired; 100-day plan execution begins on Day 1

blocks.text: Initial Screening and the Investment Thesis

After identifying an opportunity, the deal team conducts an initial screen to determine if the investment fits the fund's mandate. Most PE firms have explicit criteria around deal size (e.g., 'companies with $20-200M in EBITDA'), sector focus, geography, and return requirements. The initial screen typically takes 1-2 weeks and culminates in an investment thesis — a 2-3 sentence statement of why this company, at this price, at this time, will generate the required return.

blocks.title: Example Investment Thesis
blocks.text: Target: Regional veterinary clinic chain with $30M EBITDA. Thesis: 'We believe the US veterinary services market is highly fragmented and structurally growing due to rising pet ownership and humanization of pets. We can acquire Regional Vet at 8x EBITDA, execute a buy-and-build strategy acquiring 20+ independent clinics over 5 years, achieve cost synergies through group purchasing and back-office consolidation, and exit at 12-14x EBITDA to a strategic acquirer or larger PE firm, generating a 3.5-4.0x MOIC and 25%+ IRR.'
The Investment Memorandum Structure

For every serious deal, the deal team prepares an Investment Memorandum (IM) — a comprehensive internal document presented to the Investment Committee. Understanding the IM structure is important for interviews: it shows you understand how PE firms think about investment decisions systematically. A well-organized IM typically runs 40-80 pages and covers:

items: Executive Summary: Investment thesis in 3-5 bullets; entry/exit metrics; summary return analysis — this page must stand alone.
Industry Analysis: Market size and growth; competitive dynamics; key secular trends driving the thesis; barriers to entry; risks to the industry.
Company Overview: Business description; revenue segments; customer analysis (concentration, churn, NPS); go-to-market model; unit economics.
Financial Analysis: Historical P&L (3-5 years), normalized EBITDA bridge, working capital analysis, capex requirements, free cash flow conversion.
LBO Model: Full 5-year projection model; sensitivity tables on entry multiple, exit multiple, and EBITDA growth; return analysis (IRR, MOIC) under base, bull, and bear cases.
Value Creation Plan: Specific 100-day, Year 1, and Year 3 operational initiatives; revenue growth levers; margin improvement opportunities; M&A pipeline.
Management Team Assessment: CEO and CFO backgrounds; track record with PE ownership; retention plan; any replacements planned.
Risk Factors and Mitigants: Top 5-10 investment risks with specific mitigation strategies; deal breakers identified during diligence.
Exit Analysis: Likely exit route (strategic, IPO, SBO); comparable exit multiples; potential strategic acquirers identified; timeline to exit.

blocks.text: Due Diligence: Validating the Thesis

Due diligence is the comprehensive investigative process PE firms undertake before committing capital. It is divided into two phases: exploratory diligence (pre-LOI, high-level confirmation that the opportunity is viable) and confirmatory diligence (post-LOI, detailed verification of every material fact). A full due diligence process for a mid-market buyout typically takes 6-12 weeks and costs $1-3M in advisor fees.

blocks.caption: Private Equity Due Diligence Work Streams
rows: Is the market growing? Is the company well-positioned? What are competitive threats? Customer concentration?
Are historical financials accurate? What are normalized EBITDA and cash flows? Working capital dynamics? Quality of earnings?
Are there pending litigations? IP ownership clear? Contracts assignable? Regulatory risks?
Is the CEO capable? Is the team aligned with PE ownership? Retention plan?
PE deal team + executive search firms
Are systems scalable? What's the tech stack? Any cybersecurity risks?
Environmental liabilities? Governance red flags? Labor practices?

blocks.text: The Investment Committee

Every significant investment decision in a PE firm must be approved by the Investment Committee (IC), a group of senior partners who scrutinize deals before capital is committed. The IC process typically involves multiple touch points: an initial screening presentation (go/no-go to spend further resources), a first-round bid authorization, a final IC approval before signing, and sometimes a post-signing update before closing.

The deal team prepares an Investment Memorandum (IM) or 'deal memo' — a comprehensive document typically 40-80 pages — that includes the executive summary and investment thesis, industry and competitive analysis, company overview, financial analysis and LBO model, value creation plan, risk factors and mitigants, management team assessment, and the proposed deal structure and returns analysis.
Deal Financing: Working with Lenders

Most PE buyouts are leveraged — meaning the PE firm uses borrowed money (debt) to finance a significant portion of the purchase price. In a typical LBO, debt represents 50-70% of the total acquisition price, with equity making up the remainder. The use of leverage amplifies returns: if a company is acquired for $500M (60% debt, 40% equity = $200M equity), and sold for $700M after paying down debt to $250M, the equity value grew from $200M to $450M — a 2.25x return — even though enterprise value only grew 40%.

PE firms work with leveraged finance bankers (from banks like JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Credit Suisse) to structure and syndicate the debt. The debt package for a typical LBO includes a senior secured term loan (Term Loan B), a revolving credit facility (revolver), and potentially high-yield bonds or mezzanine debt for more aggressive capital structures.
Negotiating the Purchase Agreement

Once due diligence is complete and IC approval is obtained, the PE firm negotiates and signs the Stock Purchase Agreement (SPA) — the definitive legal document governing the transaction. Key negotiated provisions include: the purchase price and any price adjustments (working capital peg, earnouts), representations and warranties (the seller's factual statements about the business), indemnification provisions (who bears the cost of post-closing issues), closing conditions, and representations and warranties (R&W) insurance.
Management Incentive Plans

Aligning management team incentives with PE firm goals is a critical post-signing task. PE firms typically reserve 10-15% of the fully diluted equity for a Management Incentive Plan (MIP), also called a sweet equity pool. This equity is structured to vest over time (typically 3-5 years) and is often heavily back-ended, meaning management earns the most if they stay through the exit. Common structures include: (1) time-based vesting options or restricted shares, (2) performance-based awards tied to EBITDA or return thresholds, and (3) co-investment rights allowing management to invest their own capital at a discount.
The 100-Day Plan

Once a deal closes, PE firms execute a 100-day plan — a detailed operational roadmap for the first three months of ownership. The 100-day plan is developed during due diligence and begins implementation on Day 1 of ownership. It typically covers: establishing financial reporting and KPI dashboards, replacing or retaining key management personnel, implementing quick-win cost reductions (e.g., redundant vendor contracts, facilities rationalization), launching procurement initiatives, communicating with employees, customers, and suppliers, beginning integration of any simultaneous add-on acquisitions, and setting up governance structures (board composition, reporting cadence).

blocks.title: Interview Tip: The Full Deal Process Timeline
blocks.text: Interviewers often ask 'walk me through a PE deal from start to finish.' A strong answer covers: (1) deal sourcing — proprietary vs. banker auction; (2) initial screen and thesis; (3) management presentation and NDA; (4) LOI / term sheet submission; (5) due diligence — commercial, financial, legal, management; (6) IC approval; (7) financing commitments from lenders; (8) SPA negotiation and signing; (9) regulatory approvals; (10) closing; (11) 100-day plan execution. A typical deal takes 3-6 months from initial contact to closing.

blocks.title: Key Insight: Proprietary vs. Auction Processes
blocks.text: Top PE firms actively try to source proprietary deals because they avoid competitive auctions where prices get bid up. Firms like Vista Equity Partners have entire teams dedicated to proactive outreach. However, most sizeable deals (above $500M) involve investment bank-run processes because sellers want to maximize competitive tension. In a hot auction for a high-quality asset, PE firms may pay 12-16x EBITDA, leaving very little room for error in the value creation plan.

keyTakeaways: The PE investment process flows from deal sourcing through screening, due diligence, IC approval, financing, SPA negotiation, and closing — typically taking 3-6 months.
Due diligence is multi-stream (commercial, financial, legal, management, ESG) and costs $1-3M for mid-market deals — only undertaken after serious commitment.
The investment thesis drives everything: a clear 2-3 sentence articulation of why a company at a specific price will generate target returns.
Management Incentive Plans (10-15% equity pool) are critical for aligning management with PE objectives and retaining talent through the hold period.
The 100-day plan converts the due diligence findings into Day 1 actions — from financial reporting setup to operational quick-wins.
lessons.interviewTip: For 'walk me through a PE deal process' questions, use the acronym SDDIF: Sourcing, Diligence, Decision (IC), Investment (financing + SPA), and Flourish (100-day plan). Interviewers particularly value candidates who can articulate the distinction between exploratory and confirmatory diligence, and who understand that the IC process requires multiple approvals — not a single rubber stamp.

lessons.title: Value Creation in Private Equity

blocks.text: The Three Levers of PE Returns

Private equity returns are generated through three fundamental mechanisms, often called the 'value creation levers.' Understanding each lever — and how much each contributes to total returns — is one of the most tested concepts in PE interviews. Every dollar of equity return in an LBO can be attributed to one of these three sources: EBITDA growth, multiple expansion, and leverage paydown. Sophisticated buyers and sellers run formal 'returns attribution analysis' to decompose historical returns and project future ones.

blocks.title: Industry Shift: Where Returns Come From Today
blocks.text: Bain Capital's 2024 Global Private Equity Report found that operational improvement (EBITDA growth) now contributes the majority of PE returns for 2018-2023 vintage funds, while leverage and multiple expansion contribute meaningfully less than in prior decades. This shift reflects both more competitive markets (limiting multiple expansion) and higher interest rates (making leverage more expensive). Top PE firms today win on operational excellence, not financial engineering.

blocks.title: PE Return Attribution — Historical Average
blocks.subtitle: Contribution to total equity return across 2015-2022 vintage US buyout funds

blocks.text: Lever 1: EBITDA Growth

EBITDA growth is the primary and most durable source of PE value creation, typically contributing 40-60% of total equity returns in successful deals. Because enterprise value is calculated as EBITDA multiplied by a valuation multiple (EV = EBITDA x Multiple), every dollar of EBITDA growth creates multiple dollars of equity value. If a company trades at 10x EBITDA, a $10M increase in EBITDA creates $100M of enterprise value — all of which accrues to equity if debt levels remain constant.
EBITDA growth has two components: revenue growth and margin improvement. PE firms pursue both simultaneously, with different tactics depending on the sector and company situation.

blocks.caption: EBITDA Growth Levers
rows: Expand from 50 to 80 locations over 3 years
Raise prices 3-5% annually by shifting to value-based pricing from cost-plus
Add 20 enterprise sales reps, implement CRM, improve win rates from 20% to 28%
Enter new US regions or international markets
Launch adjacent offerings with higher margins
Renegotiate supplier contracts using scale; 2-4% COGS savings
Reduce redundant roles post-acquisition; careful not to cut muscle
Fixed costs spread over higher revenue base improves margins naturally
Move back-office functions to lower-cost providers

blocks.text: Lever 2: Multiple Expansion

Multiple expansion occurs when a company is sold at a higher EV/EBITDA multiple than it was purchased at. For example, buying at 8x EBITDA and selling at 11x EBITDA on the same $50M of EBITDA creates an extra $150M of enterprise value — purely from the market ascribing higher value to the business, not from any operational improvement. Multiple expansion is sometimes called 'buying low and selling high' at the multiple level.

Multiple expansion is partly driven by PE strategy and partly by luck (market conditions). Strategic drivers include: improving business quality (more recurring revenue, higher margins, reduced customer concentration) which commands a premium; buy-and-build strategies that create scale (larger companies trade at higher multiples than smaller ones — this is 'multiple arbitrage'); and positioning the company for the best possible exit buyer, who may pay a strategic premium.

blocks.label: Multiple Expansion Value Creation
blocks.formula: Additional EV = EBITDA at Exit x (Exit Multiple - Entry Multiple)

blocks.title: Buy-and-Build Multiple Arbitrage Example
blocks.text: A PE firm acquires a platform company in the $50M EBITDA software services sector at 10x = $500M EV. It then acquires 5 smaller bolt-on companies, each at 5x EBITDA = 5 x $20M EBITDA x 5x = $500M total bolt-on spend. Combined EBITDA: $50M + $100M = $150M. Because the combined company is now larger, more diversified, and more attractive, it exits at 13x EBITDA = $1.95B. The multiple arbitrage on the bolt-ons ($100M EBITDA acquired at 5x, valued at exit at 13x) generated $800M of additional value.
Lever 3: Leverage Paydown

In an LBO, the acquired company's free cash flows are used to repay the acquisition debt over the hold period. As debt decreases, equity value increases — even if enterprise value stays constant. This mechanism is sometimes described as 'the company buying itself' — its own cash flows reduce the debt burden, effectively transferring value from debt holders to equity holders over time.

blocks.label: Equity Value = Enterprise Value - Net Debt
blocks.formula: Equity Value = EBITDA x Exit Multiple - Remaining Net Debt

blocks.text: The leverage paydown effect is most powerful when: (1) the company has high and stable free cash flows relative to its debt load, (2) the debt is amortizing (mandatory principal payments) rather than bullet maturity, and (3) the hold period is long enough for meaningful debt to be repaid. A company with $300M of debt at acquisition that pays down to $150M by exit has created $150M of equity value from leverage paydown alone — assuming no change in enterprise value.

blocks.question: A PE firm buys a company at 8x EBITDA ($800M deal, $100M EBITDA) using $480M debt and $320M equity. After 5 years, EBITDA is $150M, debt is paid down to $200M, and the exit multiple is 9x. Calculate the total equity value at exit and decompose the return into the three value creation levers.
blocks.answer: Step 1 — Calculate exit enterprise value: Exit EV = $150M EBITDA x 9x = $1,350M. Step 2 — Calculate exit equity value: Exit Equity = Exit EV - Remaining Debt = $1,350M - $200M = $1,150M. Step 3 — MOIC: $1,150M / $320M = 3.59x MOIC. IRR (approx.) = (3.59)^(1/5) - 1 = 29% IRR. Step 4 — Decompose returns into three levers. Entry equity value: $800M - $480M = $320M. Lever 1 — EBITDA Growth: EBITDA increased $50M ($100M to $150M). Value created = $50M x exit multiple 9x = $450M of incremental EV. Since debt unchanged for this calculation = $450M flows to equity. Lever 2 — Multiple Expansion: EBITDA at exit ($150M) x (exit multiple 9x - entry multiple 8x) = $150M x 1 = $150M of incremental EV attributable to multiple expansion. Lever 3 — Leverage Paydown: Debt reduced from $480M to $200M = $280M reduction. All flows to equity. Cross-check: Entry equity $320M + EBITDA growth $450M + multiple expansion $150M + debt paydown $280M = $1,200M. Small difference from $1,150M due to interaction effects in the decomposition. For interview purposes: EBITDA growth drove the most value (~52%), leverage paydown second (~32%), and multiple expansion contributed smaller incremental lift (~17%). The lesson: even modest multiple expansion (8x to 9x) matters because it applies to a larger EBITDA base.

blocks.text: Add-On Acquisitions and Buy-and-Build

One of the most powerful PE value creation strategies is buy-and-build (also called platform and add-on). A PE firm acquires a 'platform' company in a fragmented industry, then systematically acquires smaller competitors ('add-ons') that are integrated into the platform. This strategy creates value through: (1) multiple arbitrage (smaller companies acquired at lower multiples than the exit multiple), (2) revenue and cost synergies between combined entities, (3) increased scale that improves negotiating leverage with suppliers and customers, and (4) a larger combined company that qualifies for higher exit multiples.
Management Alignment and Incentive Structures

PE value creation is impossible without aligned management. PE firms invest significant effort in structuring management incentive plans (MIPs) that concentrate upside on exit — not base salary or annual bonuses. A typical management team might earn a 3-5x return on their co-investment (equity purchased at entry) while the PE firm earns 2.5-3.5x, with management's per-dollar return higher because their equity is often structured as 'sweet equity' with favorable economics. Retaining key executives through the exit is so important that most PE deals include 'golden handcuffs' — equity that forfeits entirely if the executive leaves before vesting.
Returns Attribution: A Real Example

Understanding how returns are attributed between the three levers is essential for PE discussions. Consider a deal where a PE firm acquires a company for $800M (8x $100M EBITDA) using 50% debt ($400M) and 50% equity ($400M). Over 5 years, EBITDA grows to $150M, the exit multiple is 10x, and debt is paid down from $400M to $200M.

blocks.title: ESG as a Value Creation Tool
blocks.text: ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) has moved from a compliance checkbox to a genuine value creation lever in PE. Well-governed companies attract better talent, face less regulatory risk, and qualify for a wider pool of acquirers (including public markets where ESG criteria matter). PE firms like TPG's Rise Fund have built entire strategies around impact investing. In interviews at ESG-forward firms, be ready to discuss how ESG factors integrate into the due diligence process and post-acquisition value creation plan.

keyTakeaways: PE returns come from three levers: EBITDA growth (40-60% of returns), multiple expansion (buying low, selling high on the multiple), and leverage paydown (debt repayment increases equity value).
EBITDA growth is the most sustainable lever, driven by revenue growth initiatives and margin improvement programs.
Buy-and-build (platform plus add-ons) is a powerful strategy combining multiple arbitrage with synergies — smaller companies acquired cheaply, combined, and exited at a premium multiple.
Returns attribution analysis decomposes what drove equity returns — essential for PE case studies and post-deal review discussions.

Modern PE returns are increasingly driven by operational improvement rather than financial engineering, as rate environments make leverage more costly and competitive markets limit multiple expansion.
lessons.interviewTip: For value creation questions, always name all three levers (EBITDA growth, multiple expansion, leverage paydown) and quantify each with the formula: Equity Value = EBITDA x Multiple - Net Debt. The best candidates then rank the levers by importance in current market conditions (EBITDA growth first, multiple expansion less controllable, leverage paydown meaningful but rate-dependent), showing they understand not just the mechanics but the current PE environment.

lessons.title: PE Returns and Exit Strategies

blocks.text: Why Exit Strategy Is Chosen From Day One

Private equity firms think about exit from the moment they acquire a company. The choice of exit path — IPO, strategic sale, secondary buyout, or recapitalization — depends on market conditions, company characteristics, fund lifecycle constraints, and the relative attractiveness of each option at the time of exit. Exit timing is one of the most important drivers of IRR: because IRR is time-weighted, exiting earlier at the same multiple generates a higher IRR than holding longer.

blocks.title: PE Exit Routes by Frequency (2020-2024 Average)
blocks.subtitle: Share of global PE exits by type

blocks.text: Exit Route 1: Strategic Sale (Trade Sale)

A strategic sale — selling to a corporate acquirer in the same or adjacent industry — is the most common PE exit route and typically generates the highest absolute valuations. Strategic buyers pay synergy premiums: they will pay for the present value of cost savings and revenue uplift they expect to achieve by combining the target with their existing business. A strategic buyer might pay 12-15x EBITDA for a business that another PE firm would only pay 9-10x for, precisely because they see $50-100M in annual synergies.

items: Advantages: Highest valuations (synergy premium), clean break (full exit for PE fund), certainty of close (strategic buyers have clearer rationale), no post-sale management complexity.

Disadvantages: Management team may be restructured post-acquisition; confidentiality concerns during process (sharing sensitive business data with potential competitors); may face antitrust/regulatory hurdles for large deals.
Best for: Companies with clear strategic fit in a consolidating industry; businesses with proprietary technology, customer relationships, or market share that strategics want.

blocks.text: Exit Route 2: Initial Public Offering (IPO)

An IPO takes the portfolio company public by listing it on a stock exchange. For PE firms, an IPO is often the most lucrative exit on paper but also the most complex, time-consuming, and uncertain. The typical PE-backed IPO process takes 6-18 months from decision to first day of trading, and even then the PE firm cannot immediately exit — they are subject to a 180-day lock-up period during which they cannot sell shares. The actual monetization for the PE fund often happens through post-IPO secondary offerings over 12-24 months.

items: Advantages: Access to public markets valuation (often highest theoretical price); creates a liquid currency for management equity; positions company for ongoing capital raising.

Disadvantages: Long process (6-18 months), high cost ($10-30M in fees), subject to market conditions ('window risk'), PE firm cannot exit immediately due to lock-up, ongoing public company costs (SOX compliance, investor relations, quarterly reporting).

Best for: Companies with $100M+ revenue, strong growth profile, clean governance, and a compelling investor narrative. IPOs are harder to execute in volatile markets — 2022's IPO market was the worst in decades.

blocks.text: Exit Route 3: Secondary Buyout (SBO)

A secondary buyout (SBO) occurs when one PE firm sells a portfolio company to another PE firm. SBOs have become increasingly common as PE dry powder (undeployed capital) has grown — in 2022-2023, SBOs accounted for approximately 40% of all PE exits globally. The selling PE fund achieves a clean exit and realizes returns; the buying PE fund sees a fresh opportunity with a new value creation thesis.

items: Advantages: Can be executed quickly and with certainty; familiar process for both parties (PE buyers are sophisticated); often allows for continued roll-up or value creation strategy.

Disadvantages: Financial buyers will not pay synergy premiums, so valuations are typically lower than strategic sales; management team may experience 'PE fatigue' from multiple ownership changes; lenders scrutinize SBOs carefully since they are buying a company already optimized by prior PE ownership.
Best for: Companies that still have meaningful growth opportunities the prior PE firm couldn't fully execute; businesses in sectors where multiple PE firms are active.

blocks.text: Exit Route 4: Dividend Recapitalization

A dividend recapitalization ('dividend recap') is not technically an exit — it is a refinancing transaction in which the portfolio company takes on additional debt and pays the proceeds as a dividend to the PE sponsor. This allows the PE firm to return cash to LPs without selling the company, often before a formal exit. Dividend recaps improve DPI (realized returns metric) and can boost IRR significantly by returning cash earlier — but they increase the company's leverage and can create risk if market conditions deteriorate.

blocks.title: Dividend Recap Example
blocks.text: PE firm buys company at $500M (50% debt/$250M, 50% equity/$250M). After 2 years, EBITDA has grown and the company can support more debt. Firm adds $150M of new debt and pays a $150M dividend to the PE fund. IRR impact: the PE fund received $150M in Year 2 and only needs to generate $100M+ at final exit (Year 5) to still achieve 2.5x MOIC. The early cash return boosts IRR from approximately 22% (without recap) to approximately 28% (with recap), even if the final exit value is the same.

blocks.title: When Dividend Recaps Go Wrong
blocks.text: The risk of dividend recaps crystallized during the 2022-2023 rate spike. Companies that had taken on additional leverage through dividend recaps in 2020-2021 (when rates were near zero) suddenly found their interest expense had doubled or tripled. A company that executed a $200M dividend recap at SOFR + 400bps in 2021 was paying ~4.1% interest ($8.2M/year). By 2023, the same floating-rate debt cost ~9.6% ($19.2M/year) — an additional $11M of annual interest burden that could tip a marginally cash-flow-positive business into distress. PE firms learned a painful lesson: recaps should only be done when the underlying business has robust, recession-resilient free cash flows, not just favorable credit market conditions.
IRR Calculation: The Core Interview Question

Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the annualized return on invested equity capital. It is the discount rate that makes the net present value of all cash flows (investment + returns) equal to zero. In PE, IRR is calculated from the perspective of the LP — accounting for the timing and magnitude of capital calls and distributions.

blocks.caption: MOIC vs. IRR by Hold Period (Approximate)

blocks.question: A PE firm bought a company for $500M (50% equity = $250M, 50% debt = $250M). It exits for $900M with $150M of debt remaining. What is the MOIC on equity? Approximate the IRR if the hold period was 4 years. Walk through every step.
blocks.answer: Step 1 — Calculate entry equity: Entry equity = Total EV - Total Debt = $500M - $250M = $250M invested by the PE firm. Step 2 — Calculate exit equity: Exit equity = Exit EV - Remaining Debt = $900M - $150M = $750M. Step 3 — Calculate MOIC: MOIC = Exit Equity / Entry Equity = $750M / $250M = 3.0x MOIC. Step 4 — Approximate IRR over 4 years: IRR = (3.0)^(1/4) - 1 = (3.0)^(0.25) - 1. Using the table: 3.0x over 5 years = 25% IRR. Over 4 years, the same 3x requires a higher IRR (money came back faster). Approximate: 3.0^(0.25) = 1.316, so IRR = 31.6%, approximately 32%. Step 5 — Decompose sources of value: Entry EV was $500M. Exit EV is $900M. EV increase = $400M. Additionally, debt was paid down from $250M to $150M = $100M debt reduction. Total equity value increase = $400M (EV growth) + $100M (debt paydown) = $500M gain on $250M investment = 3.0x MOIC. Key insight: the 80% increase in enterprise value ($500M to $900M) combined with $100M of debt paydown generated a 3x equity return — illustrating how leverage amplifies EV gains into equity returns.

blocks.title: Classic PE IRR Interview Problem
blocks.text: Setup: PE firm buys a company at 5x EBITDA, total EV = $500M ($50M EBITDA). Capital structure: 50% debt ($250M), 50% equity ($250M). EBITDA grows 10% per year. Hold period: 5 years. Exit multiple: 6x EBITDA. Debt paid down at $20M/year. Step 1 — Exit EBITDA: $50M x (1.10)^5 = $50M x 1.611 = $80.5M. Step 2 — Exit EV: $80.5M x 6x = $483M. Step 3 — Remaining debt: $250M - (5 x $20M) = $250M - $100M = $150M. Step 4 — Exit Equity: $483M - $150M = $333M. Step 5 — MOIC: $333M / $250M = 1.33x. IRR = (1.33)^(1/5) - 1 = 5.9%. Note: This is a deliberately low-return deal — 10% EBITDA growth offset by falling from 5x to 6x creates limited value when leverage isn't aggressive. Interviewers want to see if you can follow the math and diagnose why returns are modest.
A Higher-Return Example

Let's model a strong deal. PE firm acquires a software company at 8x EBITDA ($40M EBITDA = $320M EV), 60% debt ($192M), 40% equity ($128M). EBITDA grows from $40M to $70M over 5 years (~11.8% CAGR). Debt reduced from $192M to $90M. Exit multiple: 12x (premium for SaaS scale). Exit EV: $70M x 12 = $840M. Exit Equity: $840M - $90M = $750M. MOIC: $750M / $128M = 5.86x. IRR: (5.86)^(0.2) - 1 = approximately 42%. This illustrates the power of EBITDA growth plus multiple expansion plus leverage paydown working together — the holy trinity of PE returns.
GP/LP Alignment: Potential Issues

While the waterfall is designed to align GP and LP interests, several tensions can arise. Management fee bloat incentivizes GPs to grow AUM rather than returns. Fee offsets (where portfolio company monitoring fees paid to the GP are offset against management fees) are now standard in most LPAs after LP pressure in the 2010s. GP commitment amounts (1-5% of fund) are debated — LPs want high GP commitment to ensure alignment; GPs argue that their carried interest is sufficient motivation. And the increasingly common 'no-fault divorce' provisions allow LPs to remove a GP without cause with a majority vote — a powerful check on GP behavior.

blocks.title: Key Insight: The IRR Time Value Trade-off
blocks.text: IRR is time-sensitive in a way that MOIC is not. A 2.0x MOIC in 3 years is a 26% IRR; in 7 years, it is only a 10% IRR. This creates a tension for PE firms: holding longer may improve MOIC but can destroy IRR. Dividend recaps exist precisely to solve this problem — by returning cash early, the firm improves IRR without requiring a full exit. In interviews, always demonstrate you understand that PE firms optimize for IRR, not just MOIC.

keyTakeaways: The four primary PE exit routes are: strategic sale (highest value, synergy premium), IPO (complex, long, subject to lock-up), secondary buyout (clean but no synergy premium), and dividend recapitalization (return capital without exiting).
Strategic sales typically generate the highest valuations because corporate buyers pay synergy premiums; secondary buyouts are fastest but at lower prices.
IRR is calculated as (Exit Equity / Entry Equity)^(1/Years) - 1; MOIC of 3x over 5 years approximates 25% IRR.
Dividend recapitalizations improve IRR by returning capital early, but increase leverage risk — a key trade-off in the current rate environment.
GP/LP alignment mechanisms include high GP commitment (1-5%), fee offsets, preferred return / hurdle rate, and no-fault divorce provisions in LPAs.
lessons.interviewTip: Memorize the MOIC-to-IRR conversion table for 3, 5, and 7-year holds — you will need it on the spot. For the classic 'PE firm buys at X, grows EBITDA Y%, exits at Z multiple in N years — what's the IRR?' format, always solve in 5 explicit steps: (1) calculate exit EBITDA using compound growth, (2) calculate exit EV, (3) calculate remaining debt, (4) calculate exit equity, (5) calculate MOIC and convert to IRR. Show your work clearly — interviewers care about your process as much as the answer.
